---
title: "practice_3_pca_example_1"
author: "Jamie Arjona"
header-includes:
   - \usepackage{bbm}
   - \usepackage{amsmath}
   - \usepackage{mathtools}
   - \usepackage{subcaption}
   - \usepackage{bm}
date: "2024-04-17"
output: pdf_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## Biostatistics students

We have a sample of $n=100$ from biostatistics students. The study measured the 
following variables:
$X_1=\text{weight, } X_2=\text{height, } X_3=\text{shoulders width, } X_4=\text{hip width}$
(in kg. and cm.)
we got the following results:
1. $\overline{x}_1=54.25$, $\overline{x}_2=161.73$, $\overline{x}_3=36.53$, $\overline{x}_4=30.1$.
2. Covariance matrix:

$$
\begin{equation*}
        \textbf{S}=
        \begin{pmatrix}
            44.7  & 17.79 & 5.99 & 9.19  \\
            17.79 & 26.15 & 4.52 & 4.44 \\
            5.99  & 4.52  & 3.33 & 1.34 \\
            9.19  & 4.44  & 1.34 & 4.56
        \end{pmatrix}
\end{equation*}
$$
Perform an analysis of the principal components and obtain:
1. Eigenvectors and eigenvalues in a table and the variance explained by each one.
2. Select the number of components by Kaiser method or scree plot.
3. Compute the principal components.
4. Interpretation of the principal components.
```{r cars}
means <- c(54.25, 161.73, 36.53, 30.1)
covariances <- matrix(c(44.7  , 17.79 , 5.99 , 9.19,
                        17.79 , 26.15 , 4.52 , 4.44,
                        5.99  , 4.52  , 3.33 , 1.34,
                        9.19  , 4.44  , 1.34 , 4.56
                        ), nrow = 4)
```

### Eigenvectors and eigenvalues
```{r}
res <- eigen(covariances)
cat("Eigenvectors:\n")
print(res$vectors)
cat("\nEigenvalues:\n")
print(res$values)
```
### Number of components
By *Kaiser rule*:
```{r}
tot_var <- sum(diag(covariances)) # The same as sum(res$values)
cat("Total variance ", tot_var)
mean_var <- tot_var/length(res$values)
cat("\nMean variance ", mean_var)
acc <- cumsum(res$values)
cat("\nVariance by each component and accumulated variance:\n")
t(matrix(c(res$values, acc), ncol = 2))
```

By *Scree plot*:
```{r}
barplot(res$values, names.arg=1:length(res$values), 
       main = "Variances",
       xlab = "Principal Components",
       ylab = "Percentage of variances",
       col ="steelblue")
lines(x = 1:length(res$values), res$values, 
      type="b", pch=19, col = "red")
```

### Principal components:
Projection of the data into the axis defined by the eigenvectors. We will use two
components.

$$
\begin{align*}
Y_1 &= 0.8328499X_1+ 0.5029292X_2 0.1362074X_3+ 0.1867372X_4 \\
Y_2 &= 0.50952549X_1-0.85525961X_2-0.05883247X_3+ 0.07384800X_4
\end{align*}
$$
### Interpretation:
1. The first component is the variable with maximum variance with positive coefficients.
We can interpret it as *size* of the student.
2. The second component has positive coefficients with the first and forth variable and
negative with the others. We can interpret it as *form* of the student.

The first component is ordering the students by its *size*, from shorter to larger.
While the second component provides an ordering of the students by its *form*, from
smaller to bigger/athletic.

*size* and *form* are uncorrelated.

## Example: Runners
We have collected partial running times (minutes) from 12 runners in 16 km distance.
Having the runner number 5 as the fastest and the number 12 as the slowest.
The data is as follows:
![runners](img/table_runners.png)



```{r}
runners <- matrix(
  c(
    10, 10, 13, 12,
    12, 12, 14, 15,
    11, 10, 14, 13,
    9, 9, 11, 11,
    8, 8, 9, 8,
    8, 9, 10, 9,
    10, 10, 8, 9,
    11, 12, 10, 9,
    14, 13, 11, 11,
    12, 12, 12, 10,
    13, 13, 11, 11,
    14, 15, 14, 13
  ),
  nrow = 12,
  ncol = 4,
  byrow = TRUE
)
```

Perform an analysis of the principal components and obtain:
1. Descriptive statistics: scatterplots, means, covariances, etc.
2. Computation of the correlation matrix, plots it.
3. Eigenvectors and eigenvalues in a table and the variance explained by each one.
4. Select the number of components by Kaiser method or the scree plot method.
5. Compute the principal components.
6. Interpretation of the principal components.
7. Biplot of the data and interpretation.
8. Compute contributions and square cosine of the individuals. Interpret them.
9. Compute contributions and square cosine of the variables. Interpret them.


## Using the FactoMineR and factoextra packages

We go directly to step 3 onwards.
```{r}
library("FactoMineR") # PCA and related methods
library("factoextra") # Visualizations and others for FactoMineR methods.
res <- PCA(runners, graph = F)
```


### Eigenvalues and eigenvectors
```{r}
eigenvalues <- res$eig
print(eigenvalues)
```

### Screeplot
```{r}
fviz_screeplot(res, ncp=10)
```

### Extracting information from individuals and variables using coordinates, contributions and square cosines

#### Variables
Coordinates of the variables on the factor space
```{r}
res$var$coord
```

Squared cosines: Quality of the projected variables
```{r}
res$var$cos2
```
Correlations between variables and principal components gives us a way to interpret
the principal components:
```{r}
res$var$cor
```

Contributions of the variables to the principal components:
```{r}
res$var$contrib
```

Plot of the variables on the factor space
```{r}
fviz_pca_var(res, choix="var", col.var="contrib")
```

#### Individuals
Coordinates of the individuals on the factor space:
```{r}
res$ind$coord
```

Square cosines
```{r}
res$ind$cos2
```

Contributions to each principal component
```{r}
res$ind$contrib
```

Plot of the individuals onto the factor space:
```{r}
fviz_pca_ind(res, geom="text")
```

### Description of the dimensions through the correlations and significance level
```{r}
desc <- dimdesc(res, axes =c(1,2))
desc
```

### Biplot representation (individuals and variables together)
```{r}
fviz_pca_biplot(res, repel=TRUE)
```

### Interpreting the results
1. The first component is nearly equal through all the variables. It can be interpreted as
the time to finish the race. If we think on terms of $Y_1$, we can think about it 
as the *speed* to finish the race.
2. The second component has opposite signs between the first two coefficients and the
last two. This highlight the difference about runners that go slow at the start of the
race and then fast and viceversa. We can interpret this component as running *style*
3. *Speed* and *style* are uncorrelated, so having one *style* or another do not 
influences the *speed* in which the race is performed.
